content.text: Azure Arc Use Cases

Azure Arc extends the Azure control plane to resources running outside Azure — on-premises data centers, edge locations, and other clouds. It does not move workloads; it projects ARM governance onto them. The four main pillars are: unified management (single inventory across all locations), policy and compliance (Guest Configuration policies on non-Azure resources), hybrid operations (Azure Monitor Agent, Update Manager, VM extensions), and app platform (Arc-enabled Kubernetes with GitOps and Custom Locations enabling App Service and Azure Functions at the edge). Understanding which pillar applies helps teams pick the right Arc resource provider and extension set for each scenario.

attrs.alt: Azure Arc use case pillars: Unified Management, Policy & Governance, Hybrid Operations, App Platform

content.text: Key principle: Arc is additive — it does not replace your hypervisor, Kubernetes distribution, or SQL Server installation. It adds Azure ARM governance, RBAC, and tooling on top of what already exists.
Use Case 1: Unified Inventory and RBAC

The most common initial Arc use case is gaining a single inventory of all servers across data centers, edge sites, and cloud regions — visible in Azure Portal, queryable via Resource Graph, and subject to Azure RBAC. Once a server is Arc-registered (Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines), it appears in cost analysis with tags, supports RBAC role assignments without requiring Azure AD join or domain membership, and can be targeted by Azure Policy. This is often the 'crawl' phase before enabling extensions and governance policies.

# Use case 1: Unified inventory — query everything in Resource Graph regardless of location

# All Arc-registered resources across servers, K8s clusters, SQL, VMware
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type in (
       'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines',
       'microsoft.kubernetes/connectedclusters',
       'microsoft.azurearcdata/sqlserverinstances',
       'microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines'
     )
   | extend Location = iif(location == 'eastus', location, location)
   | extend ArcType = case(
       type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines', 'Server',
       type == 'microsoft.kubernetes/connectedclusters', 'Kubernetes',
       type == 'microsoft.azurearcdata/sqlserverinstances', 'SQL Server',
       type == 'microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines', 'VMware VM',
       'Unknown'
     )
   | project Name=name, Type=ArcType, RG=resourceGroup, OS=tostring(properties.osType),
             Status=tostring(properties.status), Tags=tostring(tags)" \
  --query 'data' -o table

# Enforce consistent tagging across all Arc resources (governance pre-requisite)
# Require 'Environment', 'Owner', 'CostCenter' tags on all HybridCompute machines
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'arc-require-tags' \
  --display-name 'Require tags on Arc servers' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/726aca4c-86e9-4b04-b0c5-073027359532' \
  --params '{"tagName":{"value":"Environment"}}' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>'

# Cost allocation: tag Arc resources with the same cost center as their RG
az resource list \
  --resource-type 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines' \
  --query '[].{Name:name, RG:resourceGroup, CostCenter:tags.CostCenter}' \
  -o table

# RBAC: grant a team read access to Arc inventory without Azure portal admin rights
# 'Azure Arc ScVmm VM Contributor' for VMware; 'Azure Connected Machine Resource Administrator'
# for servers; 'Azure Arc Kubernetes Admin' for K8s clusters
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Azure Connected Machine Resource Administrator' \
  --assignee '<team-group-oid>' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>'
Use Case 2: GitOps and App Platform on Arc-enabled Kubernetes

Arc-enabled Kubernetes (Microsoft.Kubernetes/connectedClusters) connects any CNCF-conformant Kubernetes cluster — on-premises, edge, or other cloud — to the Azure ARM control plane. Once connected, you can enable the Flux v2 GitOps extension and create FluxConfiguration resources that continuously reconcile the cluster to a git repository. Custom Locations extend this further: they map an ARM resource namespace to a Kubernetes namespace, enabling Azure App Service, Azure Functions, and Event Grid to deploy pods directly into on-premises clusters. The team's deployment pipeline remains identical — push to git, Flux reconciles, App Service ARM resource targets the custom location.

# Use case 2: GitOps (Flux v2) on Arc-enabled Kubernetes
# Deploy containerized workloads to on-premises or edge K8s clusters
# using the same git-push workflow as Azure AKS clusters.

# 1. Enable Arc for an existing K8s cluster (kubectl context must be set)
az connectedk8s connect \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --location eastus

# 2. Enable the GitOps (Flux v2) extension on the connected cluster
az k8s-extension create \
  --name gitops \
  --extension-type microsoft.flux \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster

# 3. Create a Flux configuration pointing to a git repo
#    Flux will continuously reconcile the cluster state to match the repo.
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name 'app-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --namespace 'flux-system' \
  --scope cluster \
  --url 'https://github.com/<org>/fleet-gitops' \
  --branch main \
  --kustomization name=apps path=./apps/prod prune=true sync_interval=5m

# 4. Check Flux configuration sync status
az k8s-configuration flux show \
  --name 'app-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '{Status:complianceState, SyncStatus:statuses[0].complianceState, LastSync:statuses[0].appliedAt}' \
  -o json

# 5. Create a Custom Location — maps an ARM namespace to a K8s namespace
#    Required for App Service on Arc, Azure Functions on Arc, Event Grid on Arc
CONNECTED_CLUSTER_ID=$(az connectedk8s show \
  --name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query id -o tsv)

FLUX_EXT_ID=$(az k8s-extension show \
  --name gitops \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query id -o tsv)

az customlocation create \
  --name 'edge-custom-location' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --location eastus \
  --namespace 'app-ns' \
  --host-resource-id "$CONNECTED_CLUSTER_ID" \
  --cluster-extension-ids "$FLUX_EXT_ID"

# 6. Deploy App Service on Arc — creates ARM app-service plan targeting the custom location
az appservice plan create \
  --name 'edge-app-plan' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --location eastus \
  --custom-location 'edge-custom-location' \
  --per-site-scaling \
  --sku K1

az webapp create \
  --name 'my-edge-app' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --plan 'edge-app-plan' \
  --custom-location 'edge-custom-location' \
  --deployment-container-image-name 'myregistry.azurecr.io/myapp:latest'
Use Case 3: Arc-enabled SQL Server (ESU and Defender)

Arc-enabled SQL Server (Microsoft.AzureArcData/SqlServerInstances) automatically registers when the host machine is Arc-enabled and SQL Server is installed. The primary use cases are: Best Practices Assessment (runs weekly, ships findings to Log Analytics), Extended Security Updates (ESU) for SQL Server 2012 and 2014 at no extra cost (traditional ESU requires Software Assurance), Microsoft Entra ID authentication replacing Windows Authentication for multi-site scenarios, and Microsoft Defender for SQL shipping alerts to Defender for Cloud. The ESU entitlement is particularly compelling for organizations not yet ready to migrate to Azure SQL or SQL Managed Instance.

# Use case 3: Azure Arc-enabled SQL Server
# Extend SQL Server best practice assessments, Microsoft Defender, and Entra ID auth
# to on-premises SQL Server 2012–2022 instances without migrating to Azure SQL.

# Arc SQL Server is automatically registered when the host machine is Arc-enabled
# and SQL Server is installed. Verify the SQL instance resource appeared:
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.azurearcdata/sqlserverinstances'
   | project Name=name, Edition=tostring(properties.edition),
             Version=tostring(properties.version),
             LicenseType=tostring(properties.licenseType),
             BestPracticesStatus=tostring(properties.assessmentSettings.enabled)" \
  --query 'data' -o table

# Enable Best Practices Assessment — runs on a schedule, reports to Log Analytics
az sql server arc assessment create \
  --name '<sql-instance-name>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --workspace-id '<log-analytics-workspace-id>' \
  --workspace-resource-id '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/<ws-name>'

# Enable ESU (Extended Security Updates) for SQL Server 2012/2014 via Arc
# This is one of the key cost-saving use cases: ESUs via Arc are free.
# ESUs via traditional SA require license purchases.
az sql server arc update \
  --name '<sql-instance-name>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --license-type Paid
# Set license-type to 'LicenseIncluded' for ESU, 'PAYG' for pay-as-you-go billing

# Enable Microsoft Entra ID authentication for Arc-enabled SQL Server
# Allows Entra groups/users to authenticate without Windows Domain
az sql server arc update \
  --name '<sql-instance-name>' \
  --resource-group '<rg>' \
  --azure-subscription-id '<sub>' \
  --azure-resource-group '<rg>' \
  --msi-client-id '<arc-server-msi-client-id>'

# Defender for SQL on Arc — enable for all Arc SQL instances via policy
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'defender-arc-sql' \
  --display-name 'Enable Defender for SQL on Arc SQL instances' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/2227c820-e27a-4c05-9a0e-4b9d9d6d5f9c' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>'

# Remediate existing Arc SQL instances not yet covered
az policy remediation create \
  --name 'defender-arc-sql-remediation' \
  --policy-assignment 'defender-arc-sql' \
  --resource-discovery-mode ExistingNonCompliant
Arc Use Case Reference
Use Case
Key Resources
Primary Benefit
Licensing Note
Unified Management
Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines, Resource Graph
Single inventory + RBAC across environments
Free (Arc agent free)
Policy Compliance
Azure Policy + Guest Configuration extension
Audit/enforce CIS, NIST, PCI across hybrid
Defender for Servers Plan 1 or 2
Patch Management
Azure Update Manager, Arc agent
Scheduled patching without WSUS/SCCM
Included with Arc registration
Monitoring
Azure Monitor Agent, DCR, Log Analytics
Unified alerting across hybrid/multi-cloud
Log Analytics ingestion costs apply
GitOps (K8s)
Arc-enabled K8s, Flux v2, Custom Locations
Same git-push pipeline as AKS for edge/on-prem
Free for standard K8s; App Svc/Functions extra
Arc SQL (ESU)
Microsoft.AzureArcData/SqlServerInstances
Free ESUs for SQL 2012/2014; Entra ID auth
ESU via Arc is free vs paid traditional ESU
Common Arc Use Case Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Treating Arc as a migration tool
Teams skip Arc because 'we'll migrate to Azure eventually'
Arc is for governance while resources stay on-prem — migration and Arc are complementary
Using Arc without Defender for Servers
Arc servers appear in inventory but have no threat detection
Enable Defender for Servers Plan 2 via Security Center policy
Deploying App Service on Arc without Custom Location
ARM cannot target the K8s namespace for app deployment
Create Custom Location first; App Svc plan must reference it
Using Arc-enabled K8s without GitOps
Manual kubectl applies are not tracked or reconciled
Enable Flux v2 extension and create a FluxConfiguration resource
Expecting Arc to replace VMware vCenter
Arc extends ARM control plane but does not replace hypervisor management
Arc coexists with vCenter — it adds Azure governance on top
Arc SQL ESU on non-Arc-registered host
ESU entitlement does not activate — host must be Arc-registered first
Ensure the SQL Server host machine has status Connected before enabling ESU
Use Case 4: Arc-enabled VMware vSphere

Arc-enabled VMware vSphere (Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere) uses a lightweight resource bridge VM deployed to vCenter that bridges the vCenter API to Azure Resource Manager. Once the bridge is running and vCenter is connected, vSphere VMs appear as ARM resources — operators can start/stop/resize them from the Azure portal or CLI, self-service VM provisioning can be delegated via RBAC without granting vCenter access, and Azure Policy via Guest Configuration applies to VMware VMs the same way as bare-metal Arc servers. This is the primary Arc pattern for organizations standardizing on Azure governance but not yet migrating from VMware.

# Use case 4: Azure Arc-enabled VMware vSphere
# Projects vCenter VMs into ARM as Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere/virtualMachines.
# Enables self-service VM provisioning from Azure Portal without vSphere admin access.

# Prerequisites: vCenter 7.0+ and Azure Arc VMware resource bridge appliance VM.
# The resource bridge is a lightweight VM that bridges vCenter API to Azure.

# 1. Deploy the Arc resource bridge OVA to vSphere (one-time setup per vCenter)
# This uses az arcappliance — a separate CLI extension
az extension add --name arcappliance
az arcappliance createconfig vmware \
  --resource-group 'arc-vmware-rg' \
  --name 'arc-vmware-bridge' \
  --location eastus \
  --vcenter-address 'vcenter.contoso.local' \
  --vcenter-username 'arc-service@vsphere.local' \
  --vcenter-password '<vcenter-password>'

az arcappliance deploy vmware \
  --resource-group 'arc-vmware-rg' \
  --name 'arc-vmware-bridge'

# 2. Connect vCenter to Arc — creates Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere/vcenters resource
az connectedvmwarevsphere vcenter connect \
  --resource-group 'arc-vmware-rg' \
  --name 'vcenter-prod' \
  --location eastus \
  --vcenter-id '<vcenter-moid>' \
  --fqdn 'vcenter.contoso.local' \
  --port 443 \
  --username 'arc-service@vsphere.local' \
  --password '<vcenter-password>'

# 3. Enable Azure management on specific vSphere VMs
#    This installs the Arc agent (azcmagent) on the VM via the resource bridge.
#    No SSH/WinRM required — vCenter handles the agent push.
az connectedvmwarevsphere vm enable-guest-agent \
  --resource-group 'arc-vmware-rg' \
  --name 'vm-webprod01' \
  --subscription '<sub>'

# 4. Create VM via ARM (self-service provisioning from Azure Portal or CLI)
az connectedvmwarevsphere vm create \
  --resource-group 'app-vms-rg' \
  --name 'vm-newapp01' \
  --location eastus \
  --vcenter-id '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/arc-vmware-rg/providers/Microsoft.ConnectedVMwarevSphere/vcenters/vcenter-prod' \
  --resource-pool-id '<resource-pool-moid>' \
  --datastore-id '<datastore-moid>' \
  --template-id '<vm-template-moid>' \
  --num-cpus 4 \
  --memory-size 8192 \
  --network-interfaces '[{"name":"nic0","networkId":"<network-moid>","ipSettings":{"allocationMethod":"dynamic"}}]'

# 5. List all Arc VMware VMs and their power state
az graph query -q \
  "Resources
   | where type == 'microsoft.connectedvmwarevsphere/virtualmachines'
   | project Name=name, RG=resourceGroup,
             PowerState=tostring(properties.powerState),
             NumCPU=toint(properties.hardwareProfile.numCPUs),
             MemGB=toint(properties.hardwareProfile.memorySizeMB)/1024,
             GuestAgent=tostring(properties.guestAgentProfile.status)" \
  --query 'data' -o table

# 6. Apply Azure Policy to VMware VMs via Arc (same policy as for Arc servers)
#    Guest Configuration extension required — enabled by enable-guest-agent step above
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'vmware-arc-nist-audit' \
  --display-name 'NIST SP 800-53 audit on VMware Arc VMs' \
  --policy-set-definition '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/cf25b9c1-bd23-4eb6-bd2c-f4f3ac644a5f' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-vms-rg'
Arc Operations Health Monitoring

Centralizing Arc fleet health in Log Analytics with Azure Monitor Agent enables consistent alerting across on-premises, edge, and cloud resources using the same KQL queries used for native Azure VMs. Key signals: heartbeat gaps detect connectivity loss between azcmagent and Arc endpoints (normal heartbeat is every 5 minutes); extension installation failures surface firewall or agent version issues; Policy compliance states track Guest Configuration audit results; Defender for Cloud security alerts from Arc servers appear in the same SecurityAlert table as native Azure VM alerts — no separate on-premises SIEM integration needed.

// Arc operational health KQL — run in Azure Monitor Logs / Log Analytics workspace

// 1. Arc server heartbeat gap: servers not seen for > 30 minutes
Heartbeat
| where TimeGenerated > ago(2h)
| where Category == 'Azure Monitor Agent'
| summarize LastHeartbeat=max(TimeGenerated) by Computer, _ResourceId
| where LastHeartbeat < ago(30m)
| extend MinutesSilent = datetime_diff('minute', now(), LastHeartbeat)
| project Computer, LastHeartbeat, MinutesSilent, ResourceId=_ResourceId
| sort by MinutesSilent desc

// 2. Update Manager: patches missing (Critical + Security) across Arc fleet
AzureActivity
| where OperationNameValue == 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/assessPatches/action'
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Success'
| project TimeGenerated, ServerName=Resource, SubscriptionId
| join kind=leftouter (
    Resources
    | where type == 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines'
    | extend PatchStatus=tostring(properties.patchSettings.patchMode)
    | project name, PatchStatus
  ) on $left.ServerName == $right.name
| project TimeGenerated, ServerName, PatchStatus

// 3. Extension installation failures across all Arc servers
AzureActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
| where OperationNameValue startswith 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines/extensions'
| where ActivityStatusValue == 'Failed'
| project TimeGenerated, ServerName=Resource, ExtensionOp=OperationNameValue,
          ErrorCode=tostring(parse_json(Properties).statusCode),
          ErrorMsg=tostring(parse_json(Properties).statusMessage)
| sort by TimeGenerated desc

// 4. Compliance state per Arc server across all assigned policies
PolicyComplianceStates
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ResourceType =~ 'Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines'
| summarize
    NonCompliant=countif(ComplianceState == 'NonCompliant'),
    Compliant=countif(ComplianceState == 'Compliant'),
    Total=count()
  by ResourceId, PolicyAssignmentName
| where NonCompliant > 0
| sort by NonCompliant desc

// 5. Defender for Cloud — security alerts from Arc servers
SecurityAlert
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ResourceType =~ 'microsoft.hybridcompute/machines'
| project TimeGenerated, AlertName, Severity, ServerName=ResourceId,
          Status, Description=trim(' ', tostring(ExtendedProperties))
| where Severity in ('High', 'Medium')
| sort by TimeGenerated desc
Summary

Azure Arc enables four use case pillars without migrating workloads: unified inventory and RBAC via Resource Graph (Microsoft.HybridCompute/machines), policy compliance via Guest Configuration on non-Azure resources, hybrid operations via Azure Monitor Agent and Update Manager extensions, and containerized app deployment via Arc-enabled Kubernetes with GitOps (Flux v2) and Custom Locations. Arc-enabled SQL Server adds Best Practices Assessment, free ESUs for SQL 2012/2014, Entra ID auth, and Defender for SQL. Each pillar adds incrementally — start with Arc registration for inventory, add extensions for operations, add Flux and Custom Locations for the app platform tier.
